scenes.title: Second-stage decoding
scenes.tagline: Vector PR #25312 — the splunk_hec source becomes composable
scenes.footnote: +2,683 / −219   ·   7 files   ·   one source, made a pipeline

beats.narration: A pull request against Vector -- the observability data pipeline. Pull request twenty-five-three-twelve.
Its subject: the Splunk H-E-C source. The change turns a fixed-shape envelope parser into a composable ingestion point -- one that can reframe and re-decode the payloads it receives.
Seven files; most of the work concentrated in a single source. An additive change, carefully kept safe.
Before this change, the splunk H-E-C source did exactly one thing: it parsed the Splunk envelope and emitted the payload inside it -- one event, as-is.
The pull request adds an optional, per-endpoint CodecConfig -- a framing choice, and a decoding choice.
That config builds an optional Decoder, drawn from Vector's standard framing-and-decoding pipeline -- the very same codecs every other source already uses.
And the V-R-L deserializer is taught to carry envelope metadata -- host, sourcetype, the auth token -- so a remap program can route on it.

scenes.heading: Opt-in, by construction

beats.narration: The new configuration surface is small. A CodecConfig is two optional fields -- a framing, and a decoding.
build_decoder turns that config into an optional Decoder. When decoding is set, it constructs one from Vector's standard pipeline...
...choosing a sensible default framing for the chosen codec, if the operator did not name one explicitly.
And if decoding is unset -- the default -- it returns nothing. No decoder; the source behaves exactly as it always has. The feature is opt-in, and the legacy path is left untouched.

scenes.heading: One envelope, many events

beats.narration: And here is the behavioral core. Before, the loop over a request's envelopes pushed exactly one event for each envelope.
After, each envelope can decode into a whole chunk of events -- a single Splunk payload fanning out into many.
The decode loop drives the codec to end-of-input, stamping the source type, the ingest timestamp, and the auth token onto every event it produces.
It also tracks whether any frame failed to decode -- the errored flag. And that flag is the linchpin of the next, subtler change.

The subtle part is acknowledgements. With a decoder in play, ack registration is deferred -- and refused outright if the codec dropped a frame. Splunk is never told a payload landed when Vector silently lost part of it.
The output schema was reworked -- built per-endpoint, then merged -- because each endpoint now decides, at runtime, whether envelope metadata overwrites decoded fields.
Which sets a precedence rule: with a decoder, decoder output wins on a field conflict. Envelope keys are inserted only where the decoder left a gap.

And the metadata hook was added to the generic codecs crate -- so any future source, not only splunk H-E-C, can feed per-request context into a V-R-L decoder. Meanwhile the no-decoder path stays byte-for-byte the old behavior.

scenes.heading: PR #25312, in four ideas
points: An optional per-endpoint codec — opt-in; the legacy path untouched.
One HEC payload can now fan out into many events.
Acknowledgements are deferred, and refused if a frame was dropped.
The VRL decoder can route on envelope metadata and the auth token.

beats.narration: Pull request twenty-five-three-twelve, in four ideas. An optional, per-endpoint codec -- opt-in, with the legacy path entirely untouched.
One Splunk H-E-C payload can now fan out into many events.
Acknowledgements are deferred, and refused if any frame was dropped -- so a success is always honest.
And the V-R-L decoder can route on envelope metadata and the auth token. A genuinely additive feature -- the kind of change that earns trust rather than spending it.
